chapters.title: Living World and Classification of Microbes.
notes: Biodiversity means the variety of living organisms found on Earth. Living organisms differ in size, shape, food habits and habitat. To make their study easier, scientists group similar organisms together. This process is called classification.

Scientists have used different methods to classify living organisms over time. Carl Linnaeus first divided living organisms into two kingdoms, Plantae and Animalia. Later, different scientists suggested improved systems of classification. In 1969, R. H. Whittaker proposed the Five Kingdom Classification system, which is widely used today.

In 1969, R. H. Whittaker divided all living organisms into five kingdoms: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia.: He used factors like cell structure, number of cells, mode of nutrition and lifestyle for this classification.

In 1969, R. H. Whittaker divided all living organisms into five kingdoms: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia.: He used factors like cell structure, number of cells, mode of nutrition and lifestyle for this classification.
Microorganisms are the smallest living organisms and are found almost everywhere. Based on their characteristics, they are mainly classified into Bacteria, Protozoa, Fungi, Algae and Viruses.
Bacteria are unicellular microorganisms that can live independently or inside other organisms.
Protozoa are unicellular microorganisms with a well-defined nucleus.
Fungi are eukaryotic organisms that grow on dead and decaying organic matter.
Algae are mostly found in water and contain chlorophyll, which helps them prepare their own food through photosynthesis
Viruses are extremely small and can only be seen using an electron microscope. They contain either DNA or RNA enclosed in a protein coat.

Viruses cause many diseases in humans, animals and plants. Examples include Polio, Influenza and AIDS in humans, Tomato Wilt Disease in plants and Picorna Virus infection in cattle. Some viruses, called bacteriophages, infect bacteria.

flashcards.a: Biodiversity is the variety of living organisms found on Earth.
Classification is the process of grouping similar organisms together for easier study.

flashcards.q: Who proposed the Five Kingdom Classification system?
flashcards.a: R. H. Whittaker proposed the Five Kingdom Classification system in 1969.

flashcards.q: Name the five kingdoms of living organisms.
flashcards.a: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia.

flashcards.q: Why are viruses called organisms on the border of living and non-living?
flashcards.a: Viruses can reproduce only inside living cells and cannot survive independently.

practice.q: Name two methods of sowing seeds.

practice.q: Why is manure added to soil?

practice.q: Explain Whittaker's Five Kingdom Classification.
practice.a: In 1969, Robert H. Whittaker proposed the Five Kingdom Classification to classify all living organisms on the basis of their cell structure, body organization, mode of nutrition and evolutionary relationships. According to this system, all living organisms are divided into five kingdoms namely Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia. Kingdom Monera includes unicellular prokaryotic organisms such as bacteria. Kingdom Protista consists of unicellular eukaryotic organisms like Amoeba and Euglena. Kingdom Fungi includes organisms such as yeast and mushrooms that obtain food from dead and decaying matter. Kingdom Plantae consists of green plants that prepare their own food by photosynthesis. Kingdom Animalia includes multicellular organisms that depend on plants or other animals for food. This classification made the study of living organisms more systematic and scientific.

practice.q: Write the characteristics of viruses.
practice.a: Viruses are the smallest microorganisms and are considered to be at the boundary between living and non-living organisms. They can be seen only with the help of an electron microscope and consist of genetic material, either DNA or RNA, enclosed within a protein coat. Viruses cannot carry out life processes independently and become active only inside the living cells of a host organism. They reproduce by using the machinery of the host cell and remain inactive outside it. Viruses cause several diseases in humans, animals and plants, such as AIDS, influenza, polio and tomato wilt disease.

practice.q: Differentiate between bacteria and fungi.
practice.a: Bacteria are simple prokaryotic organisms that do not have a true nucleus. They are generally unicellular and their cell wall is made of peptidoglycan. Bacteria reproduce mainly by binary fission and may be either useful or harmful. Lactobacillus and Vibrio cholerae are common examples of bacteria. Fungi are eukaryotic organisms that possess a well-defined nucleus. Most fungi are multicellular, although yeast is unicellular. Their cell wall is made of chitin and they reproduce by spores or budding. Many fungi are useful in making bread, medicines and antibiotics, while some spoil food and cause diseases. Mushroom, Penicillium and yeast are common examples of fungi.

practice.q: Explain the classification of microorganisms.
practice.a: Microorganisms are extremely small living organisms that cannot be seen with the naked eye. They are classified into five major groups based on their structure and characteristics, namely bacteria, protozoa, fungi, algae and viruses. Bacteria are unicellular prokaryotic organisms that reproduce mainly by binary fission. Some bacteria are useful in making curd and medicines, while others cause diseases such as cholera and typhoid. Protozoa are unicellular eukaryotic organisms commonly found in water and moist places. Some protozoa such as Amoeba are harmless, whereas Plasmodium causes malaria. Fungi are eukaryotic organisms that obtain food from dead and decaying organic matter and reproduce by spores or budding. Algae are simple plant-like organisms containing chlorophyll and prepare their own food through photosynthesis. Viruses are the smallest microorganisms and reproduce only inside the living cells of a host organism. The classification of microorganisms helps scientists study their structure, characteristics, usefulness and harmful effects in a systematic manner.

chapters.title: Health and Diseases
notes: Health: Health is a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not just the absence of disease.Microorganisms include bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and some algae.
Disease: A disease is a condition in which the normal functioning of the body or mind is disturbed. Every disease has its own symptoms.
Diseases are mainly classified as infectious and non-infectious diseases. They can also be classified as acute, chronic and hereditary diseases.
Infectious Diseases: Infectious diseases are caused by microorganisms and can spread from one person to another through air, water, food or insects.
Non-infectious Diseases: Non-infectious diseases do not spread from one person to another. They may be caused by improper diet, lifestyle or genetic factors.
Acute Diseases: Acute diseases last for a short period of time and usually develop suddenly. Examples include common cold and flu.
Chronic Diseases: Chronic diseases last for a long time and often require continuous treatment. Examples include diabetes and asthma.
Hereditary Diseases: Hereditary diseases are passed from parents to children through genes. Down’s syndrome is an example of a hereditary disease.
Tuberculosis (TB): Tuberculosis is caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis and spreads through air. Common symptoms include cough, weakness and difficulty in breathing.
Hepatitis (Jaundice): Hepatitis is caused by a virus and mainly spreads through contaminated water or infected blood. It affects the liver and causes yellowing of the eyes and skin.
Dysentery: Dysentery spreads through contaminated food and water. Common symptoms include stomach pain and frequent watery stools.
Cholera: Cholera is caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae and spreads through contaminated food and water. It causes severe diarrhoea and dehydration.
Typhoid: Typhoid is caused by the bacterium Salmonella typhi and spreads through contaminated food and water. It usually causes high fever, headache and weakness.
Prevention of Diseases: Maintaining cleanliness, drinking safe water, eating hygienic food and taking vaccines help in preventing many diseases.

flashcards.a: Health is a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being.
Diseases that spread from one person to another through microorganisms.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between acute and chronic diseases?
flashcards.a: Acute diseases last for a short time, while chronic diseases last for a long time.

flashcards.q: Q: How can infectious diseases be prevented?
flashcards.a: By maintaining hygiene, drinking safe water and taking vaccines.

practice.q: What is fermentation?
practice.a: A process in which microorganisms convert sugar into alcohol, e.g. used in making bread and wine.

practice.q: Explain the different modes of transmission of infectious diseases.
practice.a: Infectious diseases are diseases caused by microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi and protozoa. These diseases spread from one person to another through different modes of transmission. Some diseases spread through air when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Others spread through contaminated food and water. Certain diseases are transmitted by direct contact with an infected person or by using contaminated objects. Diseases like malaria and dengue spread through vectors such as mosquitoes. Maintaining personal hygiene, drinking clean water, eating fresh food and taking vaccinations help prevent the spread of infectious diseases.

practice.q: Differentiate between infectious diseases and non-infectious diseases.
practice.a: Infectious diseases are caused by microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi and protozoa. They can spread from one person to another through air, water, food, direct contact or insects. Examples include tuberculosis, cholera and malaria. Non-infectious diseases are not caused by microorganisms and do not spread from one person to another. They may be caused by genetic factors, nutritional deficiencies or unhealthy lifestyle habits. Diabetes, cancer and heart disease are common examples of non-infectious diseases.

practice.q: Explain the importance of vaccination.
practice.a: Vaccination is the process of giving vaccines to protect the body against infectious diseases. A vaccine contains weakened or dead microorganisms that help the body develop immunity without causing the disease. Vaccination stimulates the production of antibodies, which protect the body from future infections. It helps prevent the spread of dangerous diseases, reduces the chances of epidemics and protects both individuals and the community. Vaccination has played an important role in controlling diseases such as polio, smallpox and measles.

practice.q: Explain the preventive measures for maintaining good health.
practice.a: Good health is essential for leading a happy and active life. It can be maintained by following healthy habits and preventing the spread of diseases. A balanced diet containing carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins and minerals should be consumed every day. Drinking clean and safe water, exercising regularly and getting adequate sleep help keep the body healthy and improve immunity. Personal hygiene should be maintained by bathing regularly, washing hands before meals and after using the toilet, and keeping the surroundings clean. Vaccination should be taken on time to prevent infectious diseases. Harmful habits such as smoking, tobacco consumption and alcohol should be avoided. Regular medical check-ups also help in the early detection and treatment of diseases.

chapters.title: Force and Pressure
notes: Force: A force is a push or pull acting on an object. Force can change the position, speed, direction or shape of an object.
Contact and Non-contact Forces: Forces that act only when two objects touch each other are called contact forces. Forces that act without physical contact are called non-contact forces.
Muscular Force: Muscular force is a contact force produced by our muscles. It is used in activities like lifting, pushing and pulling objects.
Frictional Force: Frictional force opposes the motion of an object and slows it down. It acts between two surfaces that are in contact with each other.
Gravitational Force: Gravitational force is the force with which the Earth attracts objects towards itself. This force causes objects to fall towards the ground.
Magnetic Force: Magnetic force is the force exerted by a magnet on magnetic materials like iron. It acts without direct contact between the magnet and the object.
Electrostatic Force: Electrostatic force is the force produced due to electric charges. A rubbed comb attracting small pieces of paper is an example of electrostatic force.
Newton’s First Law of Motion: An object at rest remains at rest and an object in motion continues to move with the same speed and direction unless acted upon by an external force.
Inertia: Inertia is the property of an object to resist changes in its state of rest or motion. Greater the mass of an object, greater is its inertia.

Pressure: Pressure is the force acting perpendicularly on a unit area of a surface. Pressure = Force ÷ Area. The SI unit of pressure is Pascal (Pa). One Pascal is equal to one Newton per square metre (N/m²).
The pressure exerted by the air surrounding the Earth is called atmospheric pressure. Atmospheric pressure decreases with increase in height above sea level.

flashcards.a: Force is a push or pull acting on an object.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between contact and non-contact forces?
flashcards.a: Contact forces act only when objects touch each other, while non-contact forces act without physical contact.
Inertia is the property of an object to resist changes in its state of rest or motion.
Pressure is the force acting perpendicularly on a unit area of a surface.
Atmospheric pressure is the pressure exerted by the air surrounding the Earth.

practice.a: Manure replenishes nutrients in the soil and improves soil fertility and texture.

practice.q: What is force? Explain its effects.
practice.a: Force is a push or a pull acting on an object. It is a physical quantity that can change the state of motion or shape of an object. The SI unit of force is Newton (N). Force can start or stop the motion of an object. It can increase or decrease the speed of a moving object and can also change its direction of motion. In some cases, force changes the shape and size of an object, such as when a rubber ball is squeezed.

practice.q: Differentiate between contact force and non-contact force.
practice.a: Contact force acts only when two objects are in physical contact with each other. Muscular force and frictional force are examples of contact forces. These forces cannot act without direct contact between objects. Non-contact force acts without any physical contact between objects. Gravitational force, magnetic force and electrostatic force are examples of non-contact forces. These forces can act even when the objects are separated by a distance.

practice.q: Explain pressure and state the factors affecting it.
practice.a: Pressure is the force acting on a unit area of a surface. It depends on both the applied force and the area over which the force acts. The SI unit of pressure is Pascal (Pa). Pressure increases when a greater force is applied on the same area. It also increases when the same force acts on a smaller area. This is why sharp knives and pointed nails work more effectively than blunt ones.

practice.q: Explain atmospheric pressure and its applications.
practice.a: Atmospheric pressure is the pressure exerted by the air surrounding the Earth on all objects. Although air cannot be seen, it has weight and exerts pressure in every direction. Atmospheric pressure plays an important role in many daily-life activities. Drinking through a straw, filling a syringe, using a dropper and sticking suction cups to smooth surfaces are all possible because of atmospheric pressure. Scientists measure atmospheric pressure using an instrument called a barometer. Knowledge of atmospheric pressure is also useful in weather forecasting.

chapters.title: Current Electricity and Magnetism
notes: Electric charge is a property of matter due to which objects attract or repel each other. There are two types of charges: positive charge and negative charge.
The electric charge produced by rubbing two objects is called static electricity. A comb attracting small pieces of paper after rubbing it with hair is an example of static electricity.
The continuous flow of electric charges through a conductor is called electric current.
An electric circuit is a closed path through which electric current flows. Current flows only when the circuit is complete.
Materials that allow electric current to pass through them are called conductors. Copper and aluminium are common examples.
Materials that do not allow electric current to pass through them are called insulators. Rubber, plastic and wood are examples of insulators.
When electric current flows through a wire, it creates a magnetic field around it. This is called the magnetic effect of electric current.

An electromagnet is a temporary magnet made by passing electric current through a coil wound around an iron core. It loses its magnetism when the current is switched off. Electromagnets are used in electric bells, cranes, loudspeakers and many electrical devices.
The region around a magnet where its magnetic force can be felt is called the magnetic field.
Magnetic field lines represent the direction and strength of a magnetic field. They move from the north pole to the south pole outside a magnet.
Current Formula: I = Q / t where: I = Current, Q = Charge t = Time, Unit of Current: 1 Ampere (A) = 1 Coulomb/second

flashcards.a: It is the production of a magnetic field around a current-carrying conductor.

practice.q: Name two methods of sowing seeds.
practice.a: Broadcasting and seed drill method.

practice.q: Why is manure added to soil?
practice.a: Manure replenishes nutrients in the soil and improves soil fertility and texture.

practice.q: Differentiate between conductors and insulators.
practice.a: Conductors are substances that allow electric current to pass through them easily because they have free electrons. Metals such as copper, aluminium and silver are good conductors and are widely used in electrical wiring. Insulators are substances that do not allow electric current to pass through them easily. Materials such as rubber, plastic, wood and glass are good insulators. They are used to cover electrical wires and protect people from electric shocks.

practice.q: What is an electromagnet? State its uses.
practice.a: An electromagnet is a temporary magnet produced when electric current passes through a coil of wire wound around a soft iron core. It behaves like a magnet only as long as electric current flows through the coil. Electromagnets are widely used in electric bells, cranes for lifting heavy iron scrap, electric motors, loudspeakers and relays. They are preferred because their magnetic strength can be controlled by changing the electric current.

practice.q: State the safety precautions while using electricity.
practice.a: Electricity is very useful but can be dangerous if handled carelessly. Therefore, proper safety precautions should always be followed while using electrical appliances. Electrical appliances should never be touched with wet hands. Damaged wires should be replaced immediately, and overloaded sockets should be avoided. The main power supply should always be switched off before carrying out any repair work. Using insulated wires and good-quality electrical equipment also helps prevent accidents.

practice.q: Explain the working of an electric circuit.
practice.a: An electric circuit is a closed path through which electric current flows from the source to an electrical device and back to the source. A simple electric circuit consists of a cell or battery, connecting wires, a switch and an electrical appliance such as a bulb. When the switch is closed, the circuit becomes complete and electric current flows through the wires. As a result, the bulb glows or the connected device starts working. When the switch is opened, the circuit breaks, stopping the flow of current and the appliance stops working. Thus, a complete circuit is necessary for the flow of electric current.

chapters.title: Inside the Atom
notes: Atom: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that retains all its properties.
Structure of an Atom: An atom consists of a central nucleus containing protons and neutrons, while electrons revolve around the nucleus in different shells.
Electron: An electron is a negatively charged particle present outside the nucleus.
Proton: A proton is a positively charged particle present inside the nucleus.
Neutron: A neutron is a neutral particle present inside the nucleus and has no charge.
Atomic Number: The number of protons present in an atom is called its atomic number.
Mass Number: The total number of protons and neutrons present in an atom is called its mass number.
Electronic Configuration: Electrons are arranged in different shells around the nucleus. The first shell can hold 2 electrons, while the second and third shells can hold 8 electrons each.
Valency: The combining capacity of an atom is called its valency. It depends on the number of electrons in the outermost shell.
Ions: Atoms gain or lose electrons to form charged particles called ions. Positively charged ions are called cations, while negatively charged ions are called anions.
Isotopes: Atoms of the same element having the same atomic number but different mass numbers are called isotopes.
Mass Number = Number of Protons + Number of Neutrons, A = P + N
Number of Neutrons = Mass Number - Atomic Number, N = A - Z

flashcards.a: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that retains all its properties.

flashcards.a: The number of protons present in an atom.
The total number of protons and neutrons in an atom.
The combining capacity of an atom.

practice.a: Broadcasting and seed drill method.

practice.a: Manure replenishes nutrients in the soil and improves soil fertility and texture.

practice.q: Explain the structure of an atom.
practice.a: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that retains its chemical properties. It consists of a central nucleus and electrons revolving around it. The nucleus contains positively charged protons and neutral neutrons. Negatively charged electrons revolve around the nucleus in fixed energy shells. Since the number of protons is equal to the number of electrons, an atom is electrically neutral.

practice.q: Differentiate between proton, neutron and electron.
practice.a: A proton is a positively charged particle present inside the nucleus of an atom. It has a relative mass of one atomic mass unit and determines the atomic number of an element. A neutron is a neutral particle also present inside the nucleus. It has almost the same mass as a proton but carries no electric charge. An electron is a negatively charged particle that revolves around the nucleus in fixed energy shells. It has a very small mass compared to protons and neutrons.

practice.q: What are atomic number and mass number?
practice.a: The atomic number of an element is the number of protons present in the nucleus of its atom. In a neutral atom, the number of electrons is equal to the atomic number. Every element has a unique atomic number. The mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus. It is obtained by adding the number of protons and neutrons. The mass number helps determine the total mass of an atom.

practice.q: Explain Bohr's model of the atom.
practice.a: Niels Bohr proposed a model to explain the arrangement of electrons in an atom. According to this model, electrons revolve around the nucleus in fixed circular paths called shells or energy levels. These shells are named K, L, M, N and so on. Electrons do not lose energy while moving in their respective shells. They absorb energy to move to a higher shell and release energy while returning to a lower shell. Bohr's model successfully explained the arrangement of electrons and helped scientists understand the structure of atoms more clearly.

chapters.title: Composition of Matter
notes: Matter: Anything that has mass and occupies space is called matter. Everything around us is made up of matter.
States of Matter: Matter exists mainly in three states: solid, liquid and gas. These states differ in the arrangement and movement of their particles.
Solid State: The particles in solids are closely packed and have strong intermolecular forces. Solids have a definite shape and volume.
Liquid State: The particles in liquids are less tightly packed and can move freely. Liquids have a definite volume but no definite shape.
Gaseous State: The particles in gases are far apart and move freely in all directions. Gases have neither a definite shape nor a definite volume.
Intermolecular Force: The force of attraction between particles of matter is called intermolecular force. It is strongest in solids and weakest in gases.
Physical Classification of Matter: Based on physical properties, matter is classified into solids, liquids and gases.
Chemical Classification of Matter: Based on chemical composition, matter is classified into elements, compounds and mixtures.
Elements: An element is a pure substance made up of only one type of atom. It cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical methods.
Compounds: A compound is formed when two or more elements combine chemically in a fixed proportion.
Homogeneous Mixture: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout the mixture. Salt solution is an example.
Heterogeneous Mixture: A heterogeneous mixture does not have a uniform composition throughout the mixture. Sand and water is an example.
Organic Compounds: Compounds that mainly contain carbon are called organic compounds. Sugar and glucose are examples.
Inorganic Compounds: Compounds that do not mainly contain carbon are called inorganic compounds. Common salt and washing soda are examples.

flashcards.a: Anything that has mass and occupies space is called matter.

flashcards.q: What are the three states of matter?
flashcards.a: Solid, liquid and gas.
A pure substance made up of only one type of atom.
A substance formed by the chemical combination of two or more elements in a fixed proportion.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between a homogeneous and a heterogeneous mixture?
flashcards.a: A homogeneous mixture has uniform composition throughout, while a heterogeneous mixture does not.

practice.q: Explain the three states of matter.
practice.a: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space. It exists in three physical states namely solid, liquid and gas. A solid has a definite shape and volume because its particles are closely packed. A liquid has a definite volume but no definite shape and takes the shape of its container. A gas has neither a definite shape nor a definite volume because its particles are far apart and move freely in all directions.

practice.q: Differentiate between solids and liquids.
practice.a: Solids have a definite shape and volume because their particles are tightly packed. They are rigid and cannot flow easily. Solids cannot be compressed under normal conditions. Liquids have a definite volume but no definite shape. They take the shape of the container in which they are kept because their particles are loosely packed. Liquids can flow easily and can be compressed slightly.

practice.q: Explain diffusion with examples.
practice.a: Diffusion is the process by which particles of one substance mix with particles of another substance due to their continuous movement. Diffusion takes place in solids, liquids and gases, but it is fastest in gases because their particles move freely. The smell of perfume spreading throughout a room and a drop of ink spreading in water are common examples of diffusion. Diffusion plays an important role in many natural processes such as respiration and the exchange of gases.

practice.q: Explain the kinetic theory of matter.
practice.a: The kinetic theory states that all matter is made up of tiny particles which are in continuous motion. These particles possess kinetic energy, and the amount of this energy increases with an increase in temperature. There are spaces between the particles, and they attract one another with different strengths. In solids, the particles are closely packed and vibrate about their fixed positions. In liquids, the particles are less tightly packed and can move past one another. In gases, the particles are far apart and move freely in all directions. This theory explains the different properties of solids, liquids and gases.

chapters.title: Metals and Nonmetals
notes: Metals: Metals are elements that are generally hard, shiny and strong. They are good conductors of heat and electricity.
Physical Properties of Metals: Metals are lustrous, malleable and ductile. They usually have high density and high melting points.
Malleability: The property of metals by which they can be beaten into thin sheets is called malleability.
Ductility: The property of metals by which they can be drawn into wires is called ductility.
Non-metals: Non-metals are elements that are generally soft and dull in appearance. They are poor conductors of heat and electricity.
Physical Properties of Non-metals: Non-metals are usually brittle and cannot be beaten into sheets or drawn into wires.
Metalloids: Metalloids are elements that show properties of both metals and non-metals. Examples include silicon, germanium and arsenic.
Noble Metals: Metals like gold, silver and platinum are called noble metals because they do not react easily with air and moisture.
Corrosion: The slow destruction of metals due to their reaction with air and moisture is called corrosion. Rusting of iron is a common example.
Prevention of Corrosion: Corrosion can be prevented by painting, oiling, greasing or coating the metal surface with another metal.
Alloys: An alloy is a homogeneous mixture of two or more metals or a metal and a non-metal. Stainless steel and bronze are common examples.

flashcards.a: The property of metals by which they can be beaten into thin sheets.
The property of metals by which they can be drawn into wires.
Elements that show properties of both metals and non-metals.
The slow destruction of metals due to their reaction with air and moisture.
A homogeneous mixture of two or more metals or a metal and a non-metal.

practice.q: Name two noble metals and explain why they are called so.
practice.a: Gold and silver; they are called noble metals because they do not react easily with air and moisture.

practice.q: Give two methods of preventing corrosion.
practice.a: Painting and oiling the metal surface.

practice.q: Name two examples of alloys.
practice.a: Stainless steel and bronze.

practice.q: State the physical properties of metals.
practice.a: Metals are generally hard, strong and lustrous substances. They have high density and high melting and boiling points. Most metals are solids at room temperature, except mercury, which is a liquid. Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity. They are malleable, which means they can be beaten into thin sheets, and ductile, which means they can be drawn into wires. They also produce a ringing sound when struck and are therefore called sonorous.

practice.q: Differentiate between metals and nonmetals.
practice.a: Metals are generally shiny, hard and good conductors of heat and electricity. They are malleable, ductile and sonorous. Most metals lose electrons during chemical reactions and form positive ions. Nonmetals are generally dull, brittle and poor conductors of heat and electricity. They are neither malleable nor ductile and do not produce a ringing sound when struck. Most nonmetals gain electrons during chemical reactions and form negative ions.

practice.q: Explain the uses of metals and nonmetals.
practice.a: Metals are used for making utensils, machinery, vehicles, electrical wires and construction materials because they are strong and good conductors of heat and electricity. Gold and silver are widely used in making jewellery. Nonmetals are also useful in everyday life. Oxygen is essential for respiration, nitrogen is used in fertilizers, chlorine is used to purify drinking water and graphite is used in making pencil leads. Sulphur is used in the manufacture of medicines and chemicals.

practice.q: Explain the chemical properties of metals.
practice.a: Metals react with oxygen to form metal oxides. Most metal oxides are basic in nature. Metals also react with dilute acids and produce hydrogen gas along with the corresponding salt. Some highly reactive metals react with water to form metal hydroxides and hydrogen gas. Metals also take part in displacement reactions, where a more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its salt solution. These chemical properties help us understand the behaviour and reactivity of different metals.

chapters.title: Pollution
notes: Pollution: The contamination of the environment by harmful substances is called pollution. It affects living organisms and the surroundings.
Air Pollution: Air pollution occurs when harmful gases, smoke and dust mix with the air. Vehicle exhaust, factories and burning fuels are major causes.
Water Pollution: Water pollution occurs when harmful substances mix with water bodies like rivers, lakes and ponds. Untreated sewage and industrial waste are common causes.
Soil Pollution: Soil pollution occurs when harmful chemicals and wastes contaminate the soil. Excessive use of fertilizers and pesticides can damage soil quality.
Noise Pollution: Noise pollution is caused by unwanted and excessive sound. Loudspeakers, traffic and industrial machines are common sources.
Causes of Pollution: Industrial activities, vehicles, deforestation and improper waste disposal are major causes of pollution.
Effects of Pollution: Pollution affects human health, damages ecosystems and harms plants and animals. It can also lead to environmental problems like global warming and acid rain.
Prevention of Pollution: Pollution can be reduced by planting trees, reducing waste, recycling materials and using clean sources of energy.
Waste Management: Proper collection, segregation and disposal of waste help in reducing pollution and protecting the environment.
Reduce, Reuse and Recycle: The 3Rs of waste management are Reduce, Reuse and Recycle. They help conserve resources and reduce pollution.
Biodegradable Waste: Waste that can be broken down naturally by microorganisms is called biodegradable waste. Food waste and paper are examples.
Non-biodegradable Waste: Waste that cannot be broken down naturally is called non-biodegradable waste. Plastic and glass are common examples.

flashcards.a: Pollution is the contamination of the environment by harmful substances.

flashcards.q: What are the main types of pollution?
flashcards.a: Air pollution, water pollution, soil pollution and noise pollution.

flashcards.q: What are the 3Rs of waste management?

practice.a: Untreated sewage and industrial waste.

practice.q: How can air pollution be reduced?
practice.a: By reducing vehicle emissions, planting trees and using clean energy sources.

practice.q: Give two examples of biodegradable and two examples of non-biodegradable waste.
practice.a: Biodegradable: food waste, paper. Non-biodegradable: plastic, glass.

practice.q: What is pollution? Explain its different types.
practice.a: Pollution is the contamination of the environment by harmful substances that adversely affect living organisms and natural resources. It disturbs the balance of nature and creates health hazards. The main types of pollution are air pollution, water pollution, soil pollution and noise pollution. Each type affects the environment in different ways and causes serious problems for human beings, animals and plants.

practice.q: Explain the causes and effects of air pollution.
practice.a: Air pollution is mainly caused by smoke released from vehicles, factories, burning of fossil fuels and forest fires. Harmful gases and dust particles mix with the air and make it unsafe for living organisms. Air pollution causes respiratory diseases such as asthma and bronchitis. It also leads to global warming, acid rain and damage to crops, forests and buildings. Continuous exposure to polluted air affects the health of both humans and animals.

practice.q: Explain the methods to control water pollution.
practice.a: Water pollution can be controlled by treating sewage and industrial waste before releasing them into rivers and lakes. Plastic waste and harmful chemicals should never be thrown into water bodies. The use of chemical fertilizers and pesticides should be reduced, and people should be encouraged to keep rivers and lakes clean. Public awareness and strict implementation of pollution control laws can help protect water resources.

practice.q: Explain the harmful effects of pollution and suggest preventive measures.
practice.a: Pollution has become one of the biggest environmental problems in the world. It affects the health of humans, animals and plants and disturbs the ecological balance. Air pollution causes breathing problems, water pollution spreads diseases and soil pollution reduces soil fertility. Noise pollution causes stress, hearing problems and lack of concentration. Pollution can be controlled by planting more trees, reducing the use of plastics, recycling waste materials and using eco-friendly products. Industries should treat their waste before disposal, and people should use public transport whenever possible. Creating awareness among people is one of the most effective ways to reduce pollution and protect the environment.

chapters.title: Disaster Management
notes: Disaster: A disaster is a sudden event that causes large-scale damage to life, property or the environment. Disasters can be natural or man-made.
Natural Disasters: Natural disasters occur due to natural processes of the Earth. Earthquakes, floods, cyclones and droughts are common examples.
Man-made Disasters: Man-made disasters are caused by human activities or negligence. Fires, industrial accidents and chemical leaks are examples.
Disaster Management: Disaster management involves planning and taking actions to reduce the effects of disasters and protect people and property.
Disaster Preparedness: Disaster preparedness means being ready to face disasters before they occur. Awareness, training and emergency planning are important parts of preparedness.
Earthquake: An earthquake is the sudden shaking of the Earth's surface caused by movements inside the Earth.
Flood: A flood occurs when water overflows and covers land that is normally dry. Heavy rainfall is one of its main causes.
Cyclone: A cyclone is a powerful storm with strong winds and heavy rainfall that forms over the sea.
Drought: A drought is a long period of very little or no rainfall, leading to water shortages.
Fire Safety: Fire safety measures include using fire extinguishers, avoiding electrical overload and following emergency exits during a fire.
First Aid: First aid is the immediate care given to an injured or sick person before medical help arrives.
Emergency Services: Police, fire brigade and ambulance services play an important role during disasters and emergencies.
Disaster Response: Disaster response includes rescue, medical aid and relief work carried out immediately after a disaster.
Rehabilitation and Recovery: Rehabilitation helps affected people return to normal life by providing shelter, food and other support.
Role of Citizens: Citizens should stay calm, follow safety instructions and help others during emergencies.
Mock Drills: Mock drills help people practice safety measures and prepare for real emergencies.

flashcards.a: A disaster is a sudden event that causes damage to life, property or the environment.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between natural and man-made disasters?
flashcards.a: Natural disasters occur due to natural processes, while man-made disasters are caused by human activities.
Disaster management involves planning and actions to reduce the effects of disasters.
First aid is the immediate care given before medical help arrives.
Earthquake, flood, cyclone and drought.

practice.q: What is the purpose of mock drills?
practice.a: Mock drills help people practice safety measures and prepare for real emergencies.

practice.q: Name three emergency services.
practice.a: Police, fire brigade and ambulance.

practice.q: What steps should citizens take during a disaster?
practice.a: Stay calm, follow safety instructions and help others during emergencies.

practice.q: What is a disaster? Explain its types.
practice.a: A disaster is a sudden event that causes severe damage to life, property and the environment. It disrupts normal life and requires immediate help and proper management. Disasters are mainly of two types: natural and man-made. Natural disasters include earthquakes, floods, cyclones, droughts and landslides, whereas man-made disasters include fire accidents, chemical leaks, industrial explosions and nuclear accidents. Both types of disasters can cause heavy loss of life and property.

practice.q: Explain the importance of disaster management.
practice.a: Disaster management is the systematic planning and organization of resources to reduce the effects of disasters. It helps in protecting people and property before, during and after a disaster. It creates awareness among people, improves preparedness and ensures quick rescue and relief operations. Disaster management also helps in rehabilitation, rebuilding damaged areas and reducing future risks through proper planning and training.

practice.q: Explain the safety measures to be followed during an earthquake.
practice.a: During an earthquake, a person should remain calm and avoid panicking. One should immediately take shelter under a strong table or desk and protect the head and neck from falling objects. People should stay away from windows, heavy furniture and electric poles. Lifts should not be used during an earthquake. After the shaking stops, everyone should move carefully to an open area and follow the instructions given by the rescue authorities.

practice.q: Explain the stages of disaster management.
practice.a: Disaster management consists of four important stages that help reduce the damage caused by disasters. The first stage is mitigation, which includes measures taken to reduce the impact of disasters through proper planning and construction. The second stage is preparedness, in which people are trained through awareness programmes and mock drills. The third stage is response, which includes rescue operations, medical aid, food and shelter during the disaster. The final stage is recovery, in which damaged areas are rebuilt and affected people are helped to return to their normal lives.

chapters.title: Cell and Cell Organelles
notes: Cell: The basic structural and functional unit of life. All living organisms are made up of cells.
Discovery of Cell: Robert Hooke discovered cells in 1665 while observing a thin slice of cork under a microscope.
Microscope: An instrument used to observe objects that are too small to be seen with the naked eye.
Cell Membrane: Protects the cell and controls the movement of substances in and out of the cell.
Cell Wall: A rigid outer layer found only in plant cells that provides shape, support and protection.
Cytoplasm: Jelly-like substance inside the cell where cell organelles are present.
Nucleus: Controls all the activities of the cell and contains hereditary material.
Cell Organelles: Special structures inside the cell that perform specific functions.
Mitochondria: Produces energy for the cell and is known as the powerhouse of the cell.
Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER): Helps in transporting materials within the cell.
Golgi Body: Stores, modifies and packages substances produced by the cell.
Lysosomes: Contains digestive enzymes that break down waste materials and damaged cell parts.
Vacuoles: Stores food, water and waste materials inside the cell.
Chloroplast: Present only in plant cells and contains chlorophyll required for photosynthesis.
Plant Cell: Contains a cell wall, chloroplasts and usually a large vacuole.
Animal Cell: Does not contain a cell wall or chloroplasts and usually has smaller vacuoles.

flashcards.a: The basic structural and functional unit of life.
Robert Hooke discovered the cell in 1665.

flashcards.q: Which organelle is called the powerhouse of the cell?
flashcards.a: Mitochondria.

flashcards.q: Which organelle controls all activities of the cell?
flashcards.a: The nucleus.

flashcards.q: Which structures are present in plant cells but absent in animal cells?
flashcards.a: Cell wall and chloroplasts.

practice.q: What is the function of lysosomes?
practice.a: Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes that break down waste materials and damaged cell parts.

practice.q: What is the function of the Golgi body?
practice.a: The Golgi body stores, modifies and packages substances produced by the cell.

practice.q: Why do plant cells have chloroplasts but animal cells do not?
practice.a: Plant cells need chloroplasts to carry out photosynthesis and prepare their own food, whereas animal cells get energy from food they consume.

practice.q: What is a cell? Explain its importance.
practice.a: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life. Every living organism is made up of one or more cells, and all life processes take place within them. Cells perform important functions such as growth, respiration, nutrition, excretion and reproduction. In multicellular organisms, different cells work together to form tissues, organs and organ systems. Therefore, the cell is known as the building block of life.

practice.q: Differentiate between plant cell and animal cell.
practice.a: Plant cells have a rigid cell wall outside the cell membrane, whereas animal cells do not have a cell wall. Plant cells contain chloroplasts for photosynthesis and usually have one large central vacuole. Animal cells do not contain chloroplasts and generally have small vacuoles. Plant cells are usually rectangular in shape, while animal cells are mostly round or irregular in shape.

practice.q: Explain the functions of the nucleus.
practice.a: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell. It contains hereditary material in the form of chromosomes, which carry genetic information from one generation to the next. The nucleus controls all the activities of the cell, including growth, metabolism and reproduction. It also regulates cell division and helps in the proper functioning of the entire cell.

practice.q: Explain the functions of different cell organelles.
practice.a: Cell organelles are specialized structures present inside the cell that perform specific functions. The cell membrane protects the cell and controls the movement of substances in and out of the cell. The cytoplasm is the jelly-like substance where most cellular activities take place. The nucleus controls all the activities of the cell and stores hereditary information. Mitochondria produce energy and are known as the powerhouse of the cell. Ribosomes help in protein synthesis, while the endoplasmic reticulum transports materials within the cell. The Golgi body modifies and packages proteins, lysosomes digest waste materials, and vacuoles store food, water and other substances.

chapters.title: Human Body and Organ System
notes: Organ: A group of different tissues working together to perform a specific function is called an organ. Examples include the heart, lungs and stomach.
Organ System: A group of organs working together to perform a major body function is called an organ system.
Digestive System: Breaks down food into simpler substances and absorbs nutrients needed by the body.
Mouth: Starts the digestion process by chewing food and mixing it with saliva.
Stomach: Churns food and mixes it with digestive juices to help in digestion.
Small Intestine: Completes digestion and absorbs nutrients into the blood.
Large Intestine: Absorbs water from undigested food and helps in waste removal.
Respiratory System: Helps in breathing and the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide.
Nose: Filters, warms and moistens the air entering the body.
Lungs: Absorb oxygen from the air and remove carbon dioxide from the body.
Circulatory System: Transports oxygen, nutrients and waste materials throughout the body.
Heart: Pumps blood to all parts of the body.
Blood: Carries oxygen, nutrients and waste materials.
Blood Vessels: Tubes that transport blood throughout the body.
Excretory System: Removes waste products from the body.
Kidneys: Filter blood and remove waste products by producing urine.
Urinary Bladder: Stores urine until it is removed from the body.
Nervous System: Controls and coordinates all activities of the body.
Brain: Controls thinking, memory and body functions.
Spinal Cord: Carries messages between the brain and different parts of the body.
Nerves: Carry signals throughout the body.

flashcards.a: A group of tissues working together to perform a specific function.
A group of organs working together to perform a major body function.

flashcards.q: Which organ pumps blood throughout the body?
flashcards.a: The heart.

flashcards.q: Which organ filters blood and produces urine?
flashcards.a: The kidneys.

flashcards.q: Which system controls and coordinates body activities?
flashcards.a: The nervous system.

practice.q: Name the organs of the digestive system in order.
practice.a: Mouth, stomach, small intestine and large intestine.

practice.q: What is the role of the lungs in the respiratory system?
practice.a: The lungs absorb oxygen from the air and remove carbon dioxide from the body.

practice.q: What is the function of the urinary bladder?
practice.a: The urinary bladder stores urine until it is removed from the body.

practice.q: What is an organ system? Explain its importance.
practice.a: An organ system is a group of organs that work together to perform a specific function in the human body. Each organ has a particular role, and together they help maintain the normal functioning of the body. Different organ systems perform different functions such as digestion, respiration, circulation, excretion and reproduction. Proper coordination among these systems is essential for growth, survival and maintaining good health.

practice.q: Explain the functions of the digestive system.
practice.a: The digestive system helps in breaking down food into simpler substances that can be absorbed and used by the body. Digestion begins in the mouth, where food is chewed and mixed with saliva. The food then passes through the oesophagus into the stomach and finally reaches the small intestine, where digestion is completed and nutrients are absorbed. The large intestine absorbs water, and the undigested food is removed from the body through the rectum and anus.

practice.q: Explain the functions of the respiratory system.
practice.a: The respiratory system helps in the exchange of gases between the body and the environment. It supplies oxygen needed for respiration and removes carbon dioxide produced during cellular activities. Air enters through the nose, passes through the trachea and reaches the lungs. In the lungs, oxygen enters the blood while carbon dioxide leaves the blood and is exhaled. This process is essential for producing energy in the body.

practice.q: Explain the circulatory system in human beings.
practice.a: The circulatory system is responsible for transporting oxygen, nutrients, hormones and other essential substances throughout the body. It also removes waste products such as carbon dioxide from body tissues. The heart acts as a muscular pump that circulates blood through arteries, veins and capillaries. Arteries carry oxygen-rich blood away from the heart, while veins return oxygen-poor blood back to the heart. Capillaries allow the exchange of gases, nutrients and waste materials between the blood and body cells. The circulatory system helps maintain the proper functioning of all organs and keeps the body healthy.

chapters.title: Introduction to Acid and Base
notes: Acid: A substance that tastes sour and turns blue litmus paper red is called an acid.
Natural Acids (Organic Acids): Acids found naturally in plants and food items like lemon, tamarind, curd and vinegar.
Mineral Acids: Strong acids such as hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) and nitric acid (HNO₃).
Properties of Acids: Acids are sour in taste, contain hydrogen ions (H⁺), react with metals and turn blue litmus paper red.
Uses of Acids: Acids are used in making fertilizers, medicines, dyes, batteries and for water purification.
Base: A substance whose water solution gives hydroxide ions (OH⁻). Bases are chemically opposite to acids.
Examples of Bases: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium hydroxide (KOH), calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)₂], magnesium hydroxide [Mg(OH)₂] and ammonium hydroxide (NH₄OH).
Properties of Bases: Bases are bitter in taste, slippery to touch and contain hydroxide ions (OH⁻).
Uses of Bases: Bases are used in making soaps, shampoos, whitewashing and fertilizers.
Indicator: A substance used to identify whether a solution is acidic or basic.
Litmus Paper: A natural indicator available in red and blue colours. Acids turn blue litmus red, while bases turn red litmus blue.
Natural Indicators: Turmeric, hibiscus, rose petals and red cabbage can also be used as indicators.
Turmeric Indicator: Remains yellow in acids and turns reddish-brown in bases.
China Rose (Hibiscus) Indicator: Turns dark pink in acids and green in bases.
Neutralization: A chemical reaction between an acid and a base that produces salt and water. Acid + Base → Salt + Water (e.g. HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O).
Uses of Neutralization: Antacids are used to reduce excess acid in the stomach, and lime is added to acidic soil to make it suitable for farming.
Neutral Substances: Substances that are neither acidic nor basic are called neutral substances. Pure water and common salt solution are examples.

flashcards.a: A substance that tastes sour and turns blue litmus paper red.
A substance whose water solution gives hydroxide ions (OH⁻).
A substance used to identify whether a solution is acidic or basic.
The reaction between an acid and a base to form salt and water.
Turmeric and hibiscus (china rose).

practice.q: What colour does turmeric turn in a basic solution?
practice.a: Turmeric turns reddish-brown in a basic solution.

practice.q: Write the neutralization reaction between HCl and NaOH.
practice.a: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O

practice.q: Give one use each of an acid and a base in daily life.
practice.a: Acid: used in batteries. Base: used in making soap.

practice.q: What are acids? State their properties.
practice.a: Acids are substances that release hydrogen ions when dissolved in water. They are commonly found in substances like lemon, vinegar and tamarind. Acids have a sour taste and should never be tasted in laboratories. Acids turn blue litmus paper red and react with metals to produce hydrogen gas. They also react with bases to form salt and water. Hydrochloric acid, sulphuric acid and nitric acid are common mineral acids.

practice.q: What are bases? State their properties.
practice.a: Bases are substances that release hydroxide ions when dissolved in water. They are bitter in taste and have a soapy feel. Strong bases can be corrosive and should be handled carefully. Bases turn red litmus paper blue and neutralize acids to form salt and water. Sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide and calcium hydroxide are common examples of bases.

practice.q: Differentiate between acids and bases.
practice.a: Acids are substances that release hydrogen ions in water and usually have a sour taste. They turn blue litmus paper red and react with metals to produce hydrogen gas. Lemon juice, vinegar and hydrochloric acid are common examples of acids. Bases release hydroxide ions in water and usually have a bitter taste with a soapy touch. They turn red litmus paper blue and react with acids to produce salt and water. Sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide and lime water are common examples of bases.

practice.q: Explain the process of neutralization and its applications.
practice.a: Neutralization is a chemical reaction in which an acid reacts with a base to form salt and water. During this reaction, the acidic and basic properties of the substances are cancelled, resulting in a neutral solution. Neutralization has many practical applications in daily life. Farmers add lime to acidic soil to improve its fertility. Antacids are used to neutralize excess acid in the stomach and provide relief from acidity. Industries also use neutralization to treat acidic or basic wastes before disposing of them, thereby reducing environmental pollution.

chapters.title: Chemical Change and Chemical Bond
notes: Physical Change: A change in which no new substance is formed is called a physical change. It is usually reversible. Examples: melting of ice, boiling of water and dissolving sugar in water.
Chemical Change: A change in which one or more new substances are formed is called a chemical change. It is usually irreversible. Examples: rusting of iron, burning of paper and digestion of food.
Characteristics of Chemical Change: Chemical changes may produce heat, light, gas, colour change or a precipitate.
Chemical Reaction: The process in which substances react to form new substances is called a chemical reaction.
Reactants: The substances that take part in a chemical reaction are called reactants.
Products: The new substances formed after a chemical reaction are called products.
Chemical Equation: A symbolic representation of a chemical reaction using chemical formulas.
Law of Conservation of Mass: Mass can neither be created nor destroyed during a chemical reaction.
Valency: The combining capacity of an atom is called valency.
Chemical Bond: The force that holds atoms together in a molecule or compound is called a chemical bond.
Molecule: A group of two or more atoms chemically combined together.
Compound: A substance formed when two or more elements combine chemically in a fixed proportion.
Formation of Chemical Bond: Atoms gain, lose or share electrons to achieve a stable electronic configuration.
Importance of Chemical Bonds: Chemical bonds help in the formation of molecules and compounds.

flashcards.a: A change in which no new substance is formed.
A change in which one or more new substances are formed.
The substances that take part in a chemical reaction.
The combining capacity of an atom.
The force that holds atoms together in a molecule or compound.

practice.q: State the law of conservation of mass.
practice.a: Mass can neither be created nor destroyed during a chemical reaction.

practice.q: Give two examples each of physical and chemical changes.
practice.a: Physical: melting of ice, dissolving sugar. Chemical: rusting of iron, burning of paper.

practice.q: How do atoms form chemical bonds?
practice.a: Atoms gain, lose or share electrons to achieve a stable electronic configuration and form chemical bonds.

practice.q: What is a chemical change? State its characteristics.
practice.a: A chemical change is a permanent change in which one or more new substances with different properties are formed. It cannot be reversed easily and usually involves a change in the chemical composition of the substance. During a chemical change, energy may be absorbed or released. Changes such as burning of paper, rusting of iron and digestion of food are examples of chemical changes. Formation of gas, change in colour or production of heat are common signs of a chemical change.

practice.q: Explain the difference between physical change and chemical change.
practice.a: A physical change affects only the physical properties of a substance such as its shape, size or state. No new substance is formed, and the change is usually reversible. Melting of ice and breaking of glass are examples of physical changes. A chemical change results in the formation of one or more new substances with different properties. It is generally irreversible and involves changes in the chemical composition of the substance. Rusting of iron, burning of wood and cooking of food are examples of chemical changes.

practice.q: What is a chemical bond?
practice.a: A chemical bond is the force of attraction that holds atoms together to form molecules or compounds. Chemical bonds are formed because atoms try to achieve a stable electronic configuration by gaining, losing or sharing electrons. The formation of chemical bonds helps atoms become more stable. The two main types of chemical bonds are ionic bonds, formed by the transfer of electrons, and covalent bonds, formed by the sharing of electrons.

practice.q: Explain the formation of ionic and covalent bonds.
practice.a: Atoms combine with one another to attain stability by completing their outermost electron shell. This combination takes place through the formation of chemical bonds. An ionic bond is formed when one atom transfers one or more electrons to another atom. As a result, positively and negatively charged ions are formed, which attract each other. Sodium chloride is a common example of an ionic compound. A covalent bond is formed when two atoms share electrons instead of transferring them. Molecules such as hydrogen, oxygen and water are formed through covalent bonding. Both types of bonds play an important role in the formation of chemical compounds.

chapters.title: Measurement and Effects of Heat
notes: Heat: A form of energy that flows from a hotter object to a colder object.
Temperature: A measure of the hotness or coldness of an object.
Thermometer: An instrument used to measure temperature.
Clinical Thermometer: Used to measure human body temperature.
Laboratory Thermometer: Used to measure the temperature of substances in laboratories.
Unit of Temperature: Temperature is commonly measured in degree Celsius (°C).
Transfer of Heat: Heat always flows from a hot object to a cold object until both reach the same temperature.
Conduction: Transfer of heat through direct contact of particles, mainly in solids.
Conductors of Heat: Materials that allow heat to pass through them easily, such as copper and aluminium.
Insulators of Heat: Materials that do not allow heat to pass through them easily, such as wood, plastic and rubber.
Convection: Transfer of heat through the movement of liquids and gases.
Convection Current: The circular movement of fluids caused by heating and cooling.
Radiation: Transfer of heat without the need for any material medium.
Good Absorbers of Heat: Dark and rough surfaces absorb heat more effectively.
Good Reflectors of Heat: Light and shiny surfaces reflect heat more effectively.
Expansion: Increase in the size of a substance when heated.
Expansion of Solids: Solids expand when heated and contract when cooled.
Expansion of Liquids: Liquids expand on heating and contract on cooling.
Expansion of Gases: Gases expand more than solids and liquids when heated.
Effects of Heat: Heat can increase temperature, cause expansion and change the state of matter.

flashcards.a: A form of energy that flows from a hotter object to a colder object.

flashcards.q: What are the three methods of heat transfer?
flashcards.a: Conduction, convection and radiation.

flashcards.q: Which instrument is used to measure temperature?
flashcards.a: A thermometer.
Dark and rough surfaces.
Transfer of heat through direct contact of particles.

practice.q: What is the difference between a clinical and a laboratory thermometer?
practice.a: A clinical thermometer is used to measure human body temperature, while a laboratory thermometer is used to measure temperatures of substances in experiments.

practice.q: Why do dark-coloured clothes feel warmer in sunlight?
practice.a: Dark surfaces are good absorbers of heat, so dark-coloured clothes absorb more heat from sunlight.

practice.q: Which expands more on heating — solids, liquids or gases?
practice.a: Gases expand the most on heating.

practice.q: What is heat? Differentiate between heat and temperature.
practice.a: Heat is a form of energy that flows from a hotter object to a colder object due to the difference in temperature. It is measured in joules and causes changes in the temperature or state of a substance. Temperature is the measure of the degree of hotness or coldness of an object. It indicates the average kinetic energy of particles and is measured using a thermometer in degrees Celsius or Kelvin.

practice.q: Explain the different modes of transfer of heat.
practice.a: Heat is transferred from one place to another by conduction, convection and radiation. Conduction takes place mainly in solids, where heat is transferred from particle to particle without the movement of the particles themselves. Convection occurs in liquids and gases through the movement of heated particles. Radiation is the transfer of heat through electromagnetic waves and does not require any material medium. Heat from the Sun reaches the Earth through radiation.

practice.q: State the effects of heat.
practice.a: Heat produces several important effects on matter. It causes an increase in temperature and may also change the physical state of a substance from solid to liquid or from liquid to gas. Heat also causes expansion of solids, liquids and gases. It speeds up many chemical reactions and plays an important role in daily life, industries and natural processes.

practice.q: Explain the uses of expansion due to heat.
practice.a: Expansion due to heat is useful in many practical applications. Metal lids of glass jars become loose when heated because the metal expands. Electric wires are kept slightly loose so that they can expand during hot weather without breaking. Expansion is also used in thermometers for measuring temperature and in the construction of railway tracks and bridges, where small gaps are left to allow expansion during summer. These applications help prevent damage caused by changes in temperature.

chapters.title: Sound
notes: Sound: A form of energy produced due to vibrations and can be heard by our ears.
Vibrations: The back and forth movement of an object is called vibration.
Production of Sound: Sound is produced when an object vibrates.
Medium: Sound requires a medium such as solid, liquid or gas to travel.
Propagation of Sound: Sound travels in the form of waves from one place to another.
Sound Cannot Travel in Vacuum: Since there are no particles in vacuum, sound cannot travel through it.
Speed of Sound: Sound travels fastest in solids, slower in liquids and slowest in gases.
Frequency: The number of vibrations produced in one second is called frequency. Its unit is Hertz (Hz).
Amplitude: The maximum displacement of a vibrating object from its mean position is called amplitude.
Pitch: The shrillness or heaviness of sound depends on its frequency.
Loudness: The loudness of sound depends on its amplitude.
Audible Range: The human ear can hear sounds with frequencies between 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz.
Infrasonic Sound: Sounds having frequencies below 20 Hz are called infrasonic sounds.
Ultrasonic Sound: Sounds having frequencies above 20,000 Hz are called ultrasonic sounds.
Echo: The repetition of sound due to reflection from a surface is called an echo.
Reflection of Sound: The bouncing back of sound waves from a surface is called reflection of sound.
Noise: Unwanted and unpleasant sound is called noise.
Noise Pollution: Excessive and unwanted sound in the environment is called noise pollution.
Effects of Noise Pollution: Noise pollution can cause stress, irritation and hearing problems.
Control of Noise Pollution: Avoid unnecessary honking, use silencers and reduce loudspeaker volume.

flashcards.a: Sound is a form of energy produced due to vibrations.
No, sound requires a material medium to travel.
The number of vibrations produced in one second.

flashcards.q: What determines the loudness of sound?
flashcards.a: The amplitude of vibrations.
The repetition of sound due to reflection from a surface.

practice.q: What is the audible range of the human ear?
practice.a: The human ear can hear sounds with frequencies between 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz.

practice.q: In which medium does sound travel fastest?
practice.a: Sound travels fastest in solids.

practice.q: What is the difference between infrasonic and ultrasonic sound?
practice.a: Infrasonic sounds have frequencies below 20 Hz, while ultrasonic sounds have frequencies above 20,000 Hz.

practice.q: What is sound? Explain how sound is produced.
practice.a: Sound is a form of energy that produces the sensation of hearing. It is produced due to the vibrations of an object. When an object vibrates, it disturbs the particles of the surrounding medium, creating sound waves. These sound waves travel through solids, liquids and gases and finally reach our ears. Since sound requires a material medium for propagation, it cannot travel through a vacuum.

practice.q: Explain the characteristics of sound.
practice.a: Sound has three important characteristics: pitch, loudness and quality. Pitch depends on the frequency of vibrations. A higher frequency produces a high-pitched sound, while a lower frequency produces a low-pitched sound. Loudness depends on the amplitude of vibrations. Greater the amplitude, louder is the sound. The quality of sound helps us distinguish between sounds produced by different sources even if they have the same pitch and loudness.

practice.q: Explain the uses of ultrasonic sound.
practice.a: Ultrasonic sound consists of sound waves having frequencies greater than 20,000 Hz. These sounds cannot be heard by the human ear but have many important applications. Ultrasonic waves are used in medical imaging to examine internal organs of the body. They are also used to detect cracks in metal objects, clean delicate instruments and measure the depth of the sea using SONAR technology.

practice.q: Explain the human ear and the process of hearing.
practice.a: The human ear is the organ responsible for hearing. It is divided into three parts namely the outer ear, middle ear and inner ear. The outer ear collects sound waves and directs them towards the eardrum. The sound waves cause the eardrum to vibrate. These vibrations are transmitted through three tiny bones in the middle ear to the inner ear. In the inner ear, the cochlea converts these vibrations into electrical signals, which are carried by the auditory nerve to the brain. The brain interprets these signals as sound, enabling us to hear different voices and sounds.

chapters.title: Reflection of Light
notes: Light: A form of energy that helps us see objects around us.
Luminous Objects: Objects that produce their own light are called luminous objects. Examples: Sun and electric bulb.
Non-luminous Objects: Objects that do not produce their own light but reflect light from other sources. Examples: Moon and planets.
Reflection of Light: The bouncing back of light after striking a surface is called reflection of light.
Incident Ray: The ray of light that falls on a surface is called the incident ray.
Reflected Ray: The ray of light that bounces back from the surface is called the reflected ray.
Normal: An imaginary line drawn perpendicular to the reflecting surface at the point of incidence is called the normal.
Angle of Incidence (∠i): The angle between the incident ray and the normal.
Angle of Reflection (∠r): The angle between the reflected ray and the normal.
First Law of Reflection: The angle of incidence is always equal to the angle of reflection.
Second Law of Reflection: The incident ray, reflected ray and normal all lie in the same plane.
Plane Mirror: A smooth and polished surface that reflects light regularly.
Image Formation in Plane Mirror: The image formed by a plane mirror is virtual, erect and of the same size as the object.
Lateral Inversion: In a plane mirror, the left side of the object appears as the right side and vice versa.
Regular Reflection: Reflection from a smooth surface where reflected rays move in one direction.
Irregular Reflection: Reflection from a rough surface where reflected rays scatter in different directions.
Multiple Reflection: Reflection of light several times from different reflecting surfaces is called multiple reflection.
Uses of Reflection: Reflection is used in mirrors, periscopes, kaleidoscopes and vehicle mirrors.

flashcards.a: The bouncing back of light after striking a surface.

flashcards.q: What is the first law of reflection?
flashcards.a: The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.
The left side appears as the right side and vice versa in a mirror image.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between regular and irregular reflection?
flashcards.a: Regular reflection occurs on smooth surfaces, while irregular reflection occurs on rough surfaces.

flashcards.q: Name two devices that work on reflection of light.
flashcards.a: Periscope and kaleidoscope.

practice.q: State the second law of reflection.
practice.a: The incident ray, reflected ray and normal all lie in the same plane.

practice.q: Describe the image formed by a plane mirror.
practice.a: The image is virtual, erect and of the same size as the object.

practice.q: What is multiple reflection? Give one example of a device using it.
practice.a: Multiple reflection is the reflection of light from more than one surface. Example: kaleidoscope.

practice.q: What is reflection of light? State the laws of reflection.
practice.a: Reflection of light is the process by which light rays bounce back into the same medium after striking a smooth surface. Reflection enables us to see objects around us and forms images in mirrors. The laws of reflection state that the incident ray, reflected ray and the normal at the point of incidence lie in the same plane. The second law states that the angle of incidence is always equal to the angle of reflection.

practice.q: Differentiate between regular reflection and irregular reflection.
practice.a: Regular reflection occurs when light falls on a smooth and polished surface. The reflected rays remain parallel to each other, producing a clear and distinct image. Mirrors are common examples of surfaces producing regular reflection. Irregular reflection occurs when light falls on a rough or uneven surface. The reflected rays scatter in different directions, and no clear image is formed. Walls, paper and rough roads produce irregular reflection.

practice.q: Explain the image formed by a plane mirror.
practice.a: A plane mirror forms an image that is virtual, erect and of the same size as the object. The image cannot be obtained on a screen because the reflected rays only appear to meet behind the mirror. The image formed is laterally inverted, which means the left side of the object appears as the right side in the image. The image is also formed at the same distance behind the mirror as the object is placed in front of it.

practice.q: Explain the applications of reflection of light.
practice.a: Reflection of light has many practical applications in daily life. Plane mirrors are used for dressing and looking at our reflection. Rear-view and side-view mirrors in vehicles help drivers observe traffic behind them and improve road safety. Reflection is also used in periscopes, kaleidoscopes, solar cookers, telescopes and many scientific instruments. It plays an important role in optical devices and helps improve visibility and observation in various fields.

chapters.title: Man made Materials
notes: Man-made Materials: Materials prepared by humans using natural resources are called man-made materials.
Polymer: A substance made up of many small repeating units called monomers.
Plastic: A lightweight, strong and durable man-made material widely used in daily life.
Properties of Plastic: Plastic is lightweight, waterproof, durable and resistant to corrosion.
Types of Plastic: Plastics are mainly classified into thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics.
Thermoplastics: Plastics that soften on heating and can be reshaped repeatedly are called thermoplastics.
Thermosetting Plastics: Plastics that cannot be reshaped once heated and moulded are called thermosetting plastics.
Uses of Plastic: Plastic is used in bottles, containers, pipes, toys and electrical appliances.
Problems Caused by Plastic: Plastic is non-biodegradable and causes environmental pollution.
Thermocol: A lightweight material used for packaging and insulation purposes.
Properties of Thermocol: Thermocol is light, soft and a poor conductor of heat.
Uses of Thermocol: It is used in packaging, disposable plates, cups and insulation materials.
Glass: A transparent and hard material made by heating sand with other substances.
Properties of Glass: Glass is transparent, hard and resistant to many chemicals.
Uses of Glass: Glass is used in windows, bottles, bulbs, mirrors and laboratory equipment.
Types of Glass: Common types include silica glass, borosilicate glass and coloured glass.
Effects of Glass on Environment: Glass is non-biodegradable and improper disposal can affect ecosystems.
Reduce, Reuse and Recycle: Following the 3Rs helps reduce the environmental impact of man-made materials.

flashcards.a: Materials prepared by humans using natural resources.
A lightweight, strong and durable man-made material.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between thermoplastics and thermosetting plastics?
flashcards.a: Thermoplastics can be reshaped on heating, while thermosetting plastics cannot.

flashcards.q: Why is plastic harmful to the environment?
flashcards.a: Because it is non-biodegradable and causes pollution.
Making windows and laboratory equipment.

practice.q: What is a polymer? Give an example.
practice.a: A polymer is a substance made up of many small repeating units called monomers. Example: plastic.

practice.q: Name two properties of thermocol.
practice.a: Thermocol is light and a poor conductor of heat.

practice.q: How can we reduce the harmful effects of man-made materials on the environment?
practice.a: By following the 3Rs — Reduce, Reuse and Recycle.

practice.q: What are man-made materials? Give examples.
practice.a: Man-made materials are materials prepared by humans using natural substances through physical or chemical processes. These materials are manufactured to possess specific properties suitable for different applications. Examples of man-made materials include plastic, glass, cement, paper, nylon and synthetic fibres. They have become an essential part of modern life because they are strong, durable and easy to manufacture.

practice.q: Explain the properties and uses of plastics.
practice.a: Plastics are lightweight, strong, durable and resistant to corrosion. They do not react easily with chemicals and are poor conductors of heat and electricity. These properties make plastics useful for a wide variety of purposes. Plastics are used in making household articles, toys, furniture, pipes, water tanks, electrical appliances, medical equipment and packaging materials. Their low cost and versatility have made them one of the most widely used man-made materials.
